text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC - FOLLOW-UP NOTE
24-Month Surveillance Visit | FIGO Stage IIIC High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient Follow-Up / Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 49-year-old Nigerian-American woman with BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC) and FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, presenting for her 24-month surveillance visit. She is currently on niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily maintenance therapy and reports feeling well overall.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Okonkwo presents in excellent spirits for her 24-month surveillance visit. She remains in complete remission by all clinical, biochemical, and radiographic parameters and has now completed 18 months of niraparib maintenance therapy (6 months remaining to complete the planned 36-month course).

Briefly, this is a 49-year-old woman who was diagnosed approximately two years ago with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3cN1M0), with diffuse peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, and large-volume ascites. CA-125 at diagnosis was 4,872 U/mL. She was found to carry a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, also known as 5382insC). She underwent primary debulking surgery (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, omentectomy, lymph node dissection, right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping, and multiple peritoneal implant resections) with achievement of complete gross resection (R0). Surgical pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma with STIC at bilateral fallopian tube fimbriae. She subsequently completed 6 cycles of adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel, during which she developed Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy and a Grade 2 carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction managed with a 12-step desensitization protocol. She achieved CA-125 normalization (11 U/mL) and complete radiographic response at end of chemotherapy. She was initiated on niraparib maintenance at the individualized starting dose of 200 mg daily (given body weight <77 kg), which was subsequently dose-reduced to 100 mg daily after Grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 98,000/mcL) at week 4 of therapy, requiring a 2-week hold followed by restart at one dose level reduction. She also developed Grade 1 anemia with concurrent iron deficiency, successfully managed with oral ferrous sulfate supplementation. At 6 months of maintenance, surveillance CT demonstrated no evidence of disease with CA-125 stable at 8 U/mL. At 12 months of maintenance (18-month disease mark), she remained in complete clinical and biochemical remission. Annual surveillance CT at the 18-month maintenance mark also demonstrated no evidence of recurrence. CA-125 has remained consistently well below 35 U/mL throughout maintenance.

At today's visit, she has no complaints suggesting recurrence. She specifically denies new or worsening abdominal distension, bloating, or change in abdominal girth. She denies pelvic pressure or pelvic pain. She denies early satiety or decreased appetite. She denies nausea or vomiting outside of manageable PARP inhibitor-associated nausea. She denies new back pain. She denies shortness of breath or pleuritic chest pain. She denies lower extremity edema. She has not noted any new palpable masses.

With regard to niraparib tolerability, she is tolerating the 100 mg dose well. She continues to take it at bedtime with ondansetron as prophylaxis and reports that nausea is now fully resolved - she has not needed to use ondansetron in the past 2 to 3 months. Fatigue is minimal, described as Grade 0 to 1, and she attributes any residual mild fatigue to the demands of her work schedule as a high school principal rather than to treatment-related causes. She reports no bleeding symptoms, no bruising, no petechiae. She notes her hair has fully grown back, which has been emotionally meaningful to her.

With regard to menopausal symptoms secondary to surgical menopause, she continues to experience some vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes 3-4 times per day, primarily daytime), which she notes have improved modestly since starting escitalopram 10 mg daily at her prior visit. She denies severe night sweats currently, though they remain present 2-3 nights per week at a manageable level. She continues to use a vaginal moisturizer (Replens) for vaginal dryness, which she finds helpful. She denies dyspareunia as she is not currently sexually active. She has no joint pain, no new musculoskeletal complaints, and no symptoms of osteoporosis fracture.

At today's visit she raises a question she has been thinking about: whether she should continue niraparib for the full 36 months or whether she could consider stopping early given her excellent response. She specifically asks whether there is data to support continuing versus what the risk of stopping would be. This will be addressed in the assessment and plan section.

Psychosocially, she has been processing significant family news. Her older child (age 22) tested positive for the same BRCA1 c.5266dupC variant and has been linked in with our genetics colleagues for enhanced surveillance and counseling regarding risk-reducing strategies including discussion of prophylactic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy at an appropriate future age. Ms. Okonkwo reports understandable anxiety about her child's result but is grateful the testing was done. Her younger child (age 19) tested negative for the variant. She has been receiving psychosocial support through oncology social work and reports that her support network remains strong. She has returned to full-time work.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats (beyond menopausal-related and improving), significant unintentional weight loss. Reports excellent appetite and stable weight. Mild fatigue Grade 0-1 as above.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations. Hypertension well-controlled on amlodipine per patient report.
Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Denies abdominal pain, distension, early satiety, nausea (resolved), vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, hematochezia, melena, change in stool caliber.
Genitourinary: Denies hematuria, dysuria. No vaginal bleeding (surgically menopausal, status post TAH). Vaginal dryness managed.
Musculoskeletal: Denies bone pain, joint swelling. Stable osteopenia under observation.
Neurological: Peripheral neuropathy essentially resolved - denies paresthesias, numbness, or tingling in hands or feet at this time (from prior Grade 2 stocking-glove neuropathy during paclitaxel, now Grade 0).
Hematologic: No bleeding symptoms, no easy bruising, no petechiae.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Vasomotor symptoms improving on escitalopram. Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine; denies symptoms of hypothyroid or hyperthyroid excess.
Psychiatric: Anxiety manageable, no active depression. Coping well.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, BRCA1-mutated (as above)
- Mild hypothyroidism - on levothyroxine, stable
- Hypertension - well-controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Surgical menopause (status post total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy)
- Osteopenia (T-score -1.4 at last DXA, 6 months ago, stable from -1.2 at 12 months)
- Iron deficiency anemia (resolved/resolving on oral supplementation)

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Primary debulking surgery (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, sigmoid mesenteric implant resection, right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping) - R0 resection achieved
- Paracentesis (diagnostic/therapeutic, prior to surgery)
- Port-a-cath placement (prior to chemotherapy initiation; port is still in situ)

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: Ovarian cancer, died age 55
- Maternal aunt: Breast cancer diagnosed age 42
- Oldest child (age 22): BRCA1 c.5266dupC positive (same pathogenic variant), under enhanced surveillance
- Younger child (age 19): BRCA1 negative

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Non-smoker, lifetime non-smoker
- Denies alcohol use
- Works full-time as high school principal (returned to full-time capacity)
- G2P2, two children, both vaginal deliveries
- Lives at home with family; strong social support network
- Nigerian-American, English-speaking

ALLERGIES:
- Carboplatin: Hypersensitivity reaction (Grade 2 - flushing, urticaria, chest tightness during cycle 4 infusion; successfully managed with desensitization protocol, cycles 5-6 completed under 12-step protocol without recurrence)

MEDICATIONS (Current):
1. Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg PO once daily at bedtime (PARP inhibitor maintenance - dose reduced from 200 mg for thrombocytopenia)
2. Amlodipine 5 mg PO once daily (hypertension)
3. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO once daily (hypothyroidism)
4. Escitalopram 10 mg PO once daily (vasomotor symptoms/mood support, surgical menopause)
5. Calcium carbonate 600 mg PO twice daily (osteopenia prevention)
6. Vitamin D3 1000 IU PO once daily (osteopenia prevention)
7. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO once daily (ongoing - iron repletion; recently reduced from BID per resolution of iron deficiency)
8. Ondansetron 8 mg PO PRN (anti-nausea prophylaxis with niraparib - using rarely now)
9. Vaginal moisturizer (Replens) - applied 3 times weekly

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Well-appearing 49-year-old African-American woman, no acute distress. Alert, oriented, and engaged. Appears well-nourished. Hair regrown to approximately 3 cm length uniformly, patient commenting positively on this.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg (well-controlled on amlodipine), Heart rate 72 beats per minute (regular), Respiratory rate 16, Temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 64.1 kg (stable, +1.1 kg from prior visit - appropriate, no concerning weight loss), Height 165 cm, BMI 23.5.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpated bilaterally.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion at the lung bases suggesting pleural effusion. No accessory muscle use.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed midline laparotomy scar without hernia or wound dehiscence. No organomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses in any quadrant. No shifting dullness, no fluid wave - no clinical ascites. Port-a-cath site: right anterior chest, no erythema, no induration, no tenderness - port appears intact and functional (patient notes port is still accessed periodically for labs per her prior preference).

Pelvic examination (external): No concerning lesions. Vaginal vault: mildly atrophic, consistent with surgical menopause. No vaginal cuff lesion palpated. No vaginal cuff tenderness. No palpable pelvic masses bilaterally on rectovaginal exam. No cul-de-sac nodularity.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness. Full range of motion.

Neurological: Grossly intact. Sensation to light touch intact bilaterally in distal upper and lower extremities - no stocking-glove deficit appreciable on exam today (prior paclitaxel neuropathy resolved). No ataxia.

Skin: No petechiae, no ecchymoses, no purpura, no rash. No jaundice. No pallor appreciated clinically.

Lymph nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

CA-125: 12 U/mL (normal; stable within normal limits, consistent with prior trend of 8-12 U/mL throughout maintenance therapy)
HE4: 52 pmol/L (normal; <140 pmol/L postmenopausal reference range)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.3 x 10^3/mcL (normal)
- ANC: 3.1 x 10^3/mcL (normal; no neutropenia)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mild improvement from prior 11.5 g/dL; recovering trend on oral iron)
- Hematocrit: 35.4%
- MCV: 84 fL (normalized from prior mild microcytosis)
- Platelets: 181,000/mcL (stable; well above the prior nadir of 98,000/mcL at time of niraparib dose reduction; no thrombocytopenia)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (GFR >60, stable baseline renal function)
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 18 U/L, Alkaline Phosphatase: 74 U/L (all within normal limits)
- Total Bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- Albumin: 4.0 g/dL

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (within normal limits; thyroid well-controlled on levothyroxine)

Iron Studies (repeat from routine monitoring):
- Ferritin: 28 ng/mL (improved from prior 12 ng/mL; iron stores replenishing on supplementation)
- Serum Iron: 72 mcg/dL (improved)
- TIBC: 340 mcg/dL (normalizing)
- Transferrin saturation: 21% (improving toward normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

Most recent surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and CT chest (performed this surveillance cycle, read by dedicated abdominal radiology): No evidence of disease. Post-surgical and post-treatment changes from prior debulking surgery are stable. No ascites. No peritoneal implants or nodularity along the pelvic peritoneum, right and left paracolic gutters, sigmoid mesentery, or omentum. No pathologically enlarged pelvic or retroperitoneal lymph nodes. No hepatic lesions. No adnexal masses (status post bilateral oophorectomy). CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. The radiologist's impression notes continued complete response with no evidence of recurrent high-grade serous carcinoma at the 24-month surveillance interval. No new findings of concern. These results were reviewed with the patient at today's visit.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO STAGE IIIC, BRCA1 GERMLINE PATHOGENIC VARIANT - COMPLETE REMISSION:
   Ms. Okonkwo is doing extremely well at 24 months from diagnosis. She is in durable complete remission across all parameters: imaging demonstrates no evidence of disease, CA-125 is stable at 12 U/mL (well within normal limits), and she is clinically asymptomatic. This represents a gratifying and expected outcome in a BRCA1-mutated patient who achieved R0 resection followed by platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitor maintenance. She has now completed 18 of the planned 36 months of niraparib maintenance. The magnitude of benefit from PARP inhibitor maintenance in patients with germline BRCA1/2-mutated ovarian cancer achieving complete response to first-line platinum is substantial, with hazard ratios for progression-free survival in the range of 0.3-0.4 in trials such as SOLO-1 and PRIMA. Completing the full planned duration remains strongly supported by available evidence.
   
   Regarding the patient's question about early discontinuation: I reviewed with Ms. Okonkwo the current data landscape. In the SOLO-1 trial, the benefit of olaparib maintenance in BRCA-mutated patients who achieved complete response was sustained out to 7-year follow-up with a significant improvement in overall survival. The PRIMA data for niraparib likewise support completing the planned maintenance duration. There is no evidence that early discontinuation in a responding patient is safe from an oncologic standpoint, and there is theoretical concern that stopping early could allow microscopic residual disease - if present - to resume growth. I acknowledge her excellent response and the burden of long-term therapy, and I emphasized that at 100 mg daily she has demonstrated good tolerability with stable hematologic parameters for many months. She heard this clearly, expressed understanding, and agreed to continue niraparib for the planned 36-month course. I will continue to reassess at each visit.
   Plan: Continue niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg PO once daily at bedtime. No dose modification indicated at this time given stable CBC and continued tolerability. CBC monitoring every 4 weeks. CA-125 and HE4 every 3 months. Next annual CT surveillance scan in 12 months (at 36-month disease mark), or sooner if symptoms develop.

2. NIRAPARIB MAINTENANCE TOLERABILITY:
   At 100 mg daily, the patient is tolerating niraparib well. Platelets remain stable at 181,000/mcL - well above the prior nadir - without any recurrence of thrombocytopenia at the reduced dose, which is a favorable and expected pattern. Hemoglobin of 11.8 g/dL reflects continued slow improvement (was 11.5 at last visit), consistent with both iron repletion effect and intrinsic hematopoietic recovery. Nausea has resolved; she no longer requires scheduled ondansetron premedication, though she has been counseled to use it PRN if nausea recurs. Fatigue is at Grade 0-1, not impairing her daily function.
   Plan: Continue as above. Repeat CBC at 4-week interval. If any hematologic changes arise (particularly platelet decline), will reassess and manage per protocol. No further dose reduction planned at this time.

3. IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA - RESOLVING:
   Iron studies confirm improved ferritin (28 ng/mL, up from 12 ng/mL) and transferrin saturation trending upward toward normal. Hemoglobin is mildly improved. Continue oral ferrous sulfate; will transition to maintenance dose (ferrous sulfate 325 mg every other day or reduced to once daily, as she has already reduced from BID per my prior note) once ferritin exceeds 30-40 ng/mL. Recheck iron studies in 3 months.
   Plan: Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO once daily. Recheck iron panel at next visit in 3 months.

4. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE / VASOMOTOR SYMPTOMS:
   Hot flashes have partially improved on escitalopram 10 mg daily. She still has 3-4 hot flashes per day and 2-3 episodes of night sweats per week, which she describes as manageable and improved from the prior visit. Given her BRCA1 status and ovarian cancer history, hormone replacement therapy is generally avoided (though some guidelines note that short-term low-dose HRT is not absolutely contraindicated following R0 resection for ovarian cancer, particularly in young women, given the LIBERATE and GOG data - however, this patient has not requested HRT and the benefit-risk discussion has not identified a compelling indication to initiate it at this time). Non-hormonal management will continue. If vasomotor symptoms worsen or substantially affect sleep quality, will consider dose escalation of escitalopram or addition of an alternative non-hormonal agent such as venlafaxine or gabapentin.
   Plan: Continue escitalopram 10 mg daily. Continue Replens vaginal moisturizer. Revisit in 3 months.

5. OSTEOPENIA:
   DXA at 18 months showed T-score -1.4 (stable from -1.2 at 12 months). She is on calcium 1200 mg daily and vitamin D 1000 IU daily. BMI 23.5 and weight-bearing activity (she walks as part of her daily routine) provide additional protection. At present, T-score is in the osteopenia range but not yet osteoporosis (<-2.5). Bisphosphonate therapy is not indicated at this time but will be reassessed at next DXA. Next DXA should be performed at the 36-month mark (one year from today).
   Plan: Continue calcium carbonate 1200 mg PO daily and vitamin D3 1000 IU PO daily. Encourage continued weight-bearing activity. Repeat DXA in 12 months.

6. HYPOTHYROIDISM / HYPERTENSION:
   Both comorbidities remain well-controlled per patient report and TSH of 1.8 mIU/L. Blood pressure 128/76 on amlodipine 5 mg - excellent control. Of note, she is not on bevacizumab (was deferred at time of treatment planning given pre-existing hypertension and patient preference), so hypertension management is straightforward. No medication changes needed.
   Plan: Continue levothyroxine and amlodipine at current doses.

7. FAMILY GENETIC IMPLICATIONS:
   The patient's oldest child (age 22) tested BRCA1 positive and is engaged with our genetics colleagues. The younger child tested negative. The patient remains understandably concerned for her older child. I acknowledged this and provided reassurance that the genetic team has the protocols in place: annual breast MRI starting now (given age and BRCA1 status), discussion of chemoprevention, and consideration of RRSO at approximately 35-40 years of age when childbearing is likely complete. The patient is grateful for the proactive management. No action required from gynecologic oncology directly.

8. PORT-A-CATH:
   Port placed prior to chemotherapy is still in situ. Given that active IV therapy is no longer ongoing and the patient is currently on oral maintenance, I had a conversation about whether to remove the port. The port has been accessed periodically for laboratory draws per patient preference. The patient expresses comfort keeping it in place for the remainder of the 36-month maintenance period, after which she would prefer to discuss removal. This is a reasonable approach. No signs of port infection or malfunction. Will revisit removal discussion at the 36-month mark or at completion of niraparib.

RETURN VISIT:
The patient will return to clinic in 3 months for routine surveillance. She will have CBC in 4 weeks (standard niraparib monitoring). She was instructed to call the office or report to urgent care/emergency department immediately for any of the following symptoms: new or increasing abdominal distension, pelvic pain, rapid weight gain, shortness of breath, significant bleeding, febrile illness, or any new symptom of concern.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 40 minutes. The patient verbalized understanding of the plan and her questions were addressed to her satisfaction.

---

SIGNED: Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
Note dictated and reviewed.
